front: Figure: the seventh president of the United States
Figure: nicknamed "Old Hickory" for his toughness
Figure: nicknamed "Sharp Knife" during his early military campaigns in Florida
Figure: also derisively called "King Mob" by opponents
Figure: the first president elected as a member of the Democratic Party
Figure: his estate near Nashville, Tennessee was called the Hermitage
Figure: gave his name to "Jacksonian democracy," the term for 1830s U.S. politics
Battle: Jackson defeated William Weatherford's Red Sticks here during the Creek War
work: Military Career: War of 1812 & First Seminole War

front: Battle: British general Edward Pakenham died storming the earthworks here after Thomas Mullins failed to provide ladders
work: Military Career: War of 1812 & First Seminole War

front: Battle: Jackson was accused in the "Coffin Handbills" (circulated by editor John Binns) of executing six militiamen after this 1815 fight
work: Military Career: War of 1812 & First Seminole War

front: Event: Jackson court-martialed and executed British subjects Alexander Arbuthnot and Robert Ambrister here for supplying arms to the Seminoles, sparking a diplomatic incident
work: Military Career: War of 1812 & First Seminole War

front: Event: Jackson won more electoral votes than John Quincy Adams here but lost the presidency in a House vote decided by the "corrupt bargain"
Event: Jackson defeated John Quincy Adams in this rematch, aided by newly expanded white male suffrage
Law: this 1830 law, signed by Jackson, forced the Cherokee and other tribes onto the Trail of Tears
work: Indian Removal Act & Trail of Tears

front: Event: after this Supreme Court case ruled Georgia lacked jurisdiction over Cherokee land, Jackson reportedly said "John Marshall has made his decision; now let him enforce it" and declined to intervene
work: Indian Removal Act & Trail of Tears

front: Figure: under his administration, a man was arrested for collaborating with Cherokee leader Elias Boudinot on a Bible translation
work: Indian Removal Act & Trail of Tears

front: Figure: his Indian removal policy was opposed by Jeremiah Evarts, who wrote essays against it under the pseudonym "William Penn"
work: Indian Removal Act & Trail of Tears

front: Event: Jackson vetoed the recharter of this institution, fighting its president Nicholas Biddle in the so-called "Bank War"
back: Second Bank of the United States
work: Bank War: Second Bank of the United States

front: Figure: Jackson appointed this man as Treasury Secretary (after firing or reassigning two prior secretaries) to withdraw federal deposits from the Second Bank
work: Bank War: Second Bank of the United States

front: Event: this Whig political cartoon depicts Jackson as a crowned figure trampling the Constitution and "internal improvements," wielding a scepter beside the words "of veto memory"
work: Bank War: Second Bank of the United States

front: Event: this political cartoon depicts Jackson as a general "slaying the many-headed monster," representing the Second Bank
back: General Jackson Slaying the Many-Headed Monster (cartoon)
work: Bank War: Second Bank of the United States

front: Document: this 1836 executive order, carried out by Treasury Secretary Levi Woodbury, required gold or silver payment for public land purchases
Event: devaluation of poorly-backed state bank paper currency, following Jackson's bank policies, helped cause this 1837 financial crisis
Law: Jackson signed this legislation authorizing federal enforcement of tariff collection against South Carolina during the Nullification Crisis
Figure: this vice president resigned over the Tariff of Abominations and the Nullification Crisis
Event: this delusional house painter, believing he was owed money as the rightful King of England, tried to shoot Jackson at the funeral of Congressman Warren Davis; both his pistols misfired
Event: Jackson beat this failed assassin with his cane, with help from Davy Crockett; he was later found not guilty by reason of insanity
Event: other cabinet wives' social shunning of Secretary of War John Eaton's wife Peggy disrupted Jackson's official cabinet
work: Cabinet Turmoil: Petticoat Affair & Kitchen Cabinet

front: Figure: Jackson relied on this informal group of advisers, including Postmaster General Amos Kendall, especially after the Petticoat affair
work: Cabinet Turmoil: Petticoat Affair & Kitchen Cabinet

front: Figure: Jackson married this woman in Natchez before her divorce from first husband Lewis Robards was legally final
Figure: opponents attacked this woman as a bigamist during the bitter 1828 campaign, via a pamphlet edited by Robert Coleman Foster
Figure: this woman died shortly after the 1828 election; Jackson blamed the attacks on her character for her death
Figure: this niece of Rachel Jackson served as White House hostess after Rachel's death, until dismissed for snubbing Peggy Eaton
Event: multiple U.S. cities, including Jacksonville, FL and Jackson, MS, are named for this president
Event: Jackson's portrait appears on this denomination of U.S. currency, which the Treasury proposed replacing with Harriet Tubman
Figure: this historian won a Pulitzer Prize for The Age of Jackson
Figure: this historian's biography Party Battles helped create Jackson's modern image, despite being largely fabricated
Figure: this historian drew on Hermitage archives for his Jackson biography American Lion
Concept: this historian introduced the "Market Revolution" thesis in a 1991 book about Jackson
Event: Jackson is linked in folklore to this haunting, said to have kicked one of his men out the door; his Hermitage may still host his ghost
Work: this rock musical portrays Jackson as its "Bloody Bloody" title character
Event: William Jennings Bryan invoked Jackson in this 1896 speech as a populist hero against the moneyed establishment
